original_hypothesis.title: Accounting Desynchronization via Rebasing Tokens
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The AMM relies entirely on internal reserve accounting rather than checking actual token balances. If a rebasing token is used, positive rebases will permanently lock the excess tokens in the contract, while negative rebases will cause the actual balance to drop below the recorded reserves, bricking pool operations.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Strict internal accounting without balance reconciliation is incompatible with elastic supply tokens.
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No deployment evidence required; vulnerability exists in core logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Internal reserve accounting (reserveUserToken, reserveValidatorToken) used instead of actual token balances

preconditions: Pool is created using an elastic supply (rebasing) token

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No mechanism exists to sync internal reserves with actual contract balances

operational_questions: Are rebasing tokens supported or expected?
Can an attacker force a rebase to intentionally brick a pool?
validation_requirements: Deploy pool with a rebasing token
Trigger a negative rebase and verify that burn() reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for or trigger a negative rebase event
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Actual contract balance drops below internal reserves
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Subsequent swaps or burns revert when attempting to transfer out tokens the contract no longer holds

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Denial of service for pool withdrawals and swaps

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Mismatch between reserves and actual balances

validation_result.impact_summary: Pools created with rebasing tokens will have internal reserves diverge from actual balances. Negative rebases brick pool operations (reverts on withdrawal/swap); positive rebases permanently lock excess tokens.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The FeeAMM contract tracks pool reserves exclusively through internal state variables (Pool.reserveUserToken and Pool.reserveValidatorToken) and updates them based on nominal transfer amounts. There is no mechanism to reconcile these internal reserves with the contract's actual token balances (no sync() function, no balanceOf checks). Rebasing tokens modify balances outside of the AMM's control flow, causing a permanent divergence between tracked reserves and actual holdings. On a negative rebase, the actual balance drops below the tracked reserves, causing all subsequent operations that attempt to transfer tokens out (burn, executeFeeSwap, rebalanceSwap) to revert due to insufficient actual balance. On a positive rebase, excess tokens accrue in the contract but are never reflected in reserves and thus are permanently inaccessible. This is a real code defect: the contract provides no recovery path and no restriction on which tokens can be used to create pools.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal at lines 20-23 correctly identifies the internal reserve struct. All state-mutating functions confirm the pattern: reserves are updated using nominal amounts (amountIn, amountOut, amountValidatorToken) without any balanceOf reconciliation. No sync or skim function exists anywhere in the contract.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Internal reserve tracking with no mechanism to sync with actual token balances. Rebasing tokens modify balanceOf without updating these variables.
Reserve is incremented by the nominal amountValidatorToken, not by the actual balance change. If the token rebases after this deposit, the internal reserve no longer matches the actual balance.

After a negative rebase, the contract's actual token balance is less than pool.reserveValidatorToken (or reserveUserToken). The transfer call will revert because the contract doesn't hold enough tokens, bricking all withdrawal operations.
After a negative rebase on validatorToken, transfer(msg.sender, amountOut) will fail because actual balance < reserveValidatorToken, even though the reserve sufficiency check passed.

calling_sequence: 1. User creates pool with rebasing token via mint(userToken, rebasingValidatorToken, amount, to)
3. External rebase event occurs (negative), reducing contract's actual balanceOf(rebasingValidatorToken)
4. User calls burn(userToken, rebasingValidatorToken, liquidity, to)
5. _calculateBurnAmounts computes amountValidatorToken = liquidity * pool.reserveValidatorToken / totalSupply (uses stale inflated reserve)
6. IERC20(rebasingValidatorToken).transfer(to, amountValidatorToken) REVERTS because contract balance < amountValidatorToken
7. Pool is bricked - no withdrawals or swaps possible
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entire reserve accounting system (lines 20-23, 105-112, 143-146, 173-178, 214-220) assumes token balances only change via the AMM's own transfer/transferFrom calls. No balanceOf check or sync mechanism exists. The contract has no way to detect or recover from external balance changes caused by rebasing tokens.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided beyond foundry.toml. Pool creation is fully permissionless with no token whitelist.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is straightforward and requires no special attacker capability beyond creating a pool with a rebasing token. (1) Attacker or innocent user creates pool with a rebasing token (e.g., stETH, AMPL, or aTokens). (2) A negative rebase occurs, reducing the contract's actual token balance. (3) All operations that transfer out the rebased token (burn, executeFeeSwap for validatorToken, rebalanceSwap for userToken) will revert because the contract tries to send more tokens than it holds. (4) LP funds are permanently locked. For positive rebases, the excess tokens are permanently trapped since no mechanism exists to account for them.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. The impact is denial-of-service for any pool using a rebasing token, with LP funds permanently locked after a negative rebase. The precondition (rebasing token used in pool) is realistic given the permissionless pool creation and no token validation. However, sophisticated users may avoid rebasing tokens, limiting real-world occurrence. The defect is in the code regardless of whether it's exploited intentionally.
recommended_mitigations: Add a sync() function that updates internal reserves to match actual balanceOf values, similar to Uniswap V2's sync()
Add a skim() function allowing excess tokens from positive rebases to be recovered
Implement a token whitelist or blacklist to prevent pool creation with known rebasing tokens
Use balance-before/balance-after pattern for all token transfers to track actual received amounts
Document clearly that rebasing tokens are not supported if the above mitigations are not implemented
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy FeeAMM and a mock rebasing ERC20 token. 2. Create pool via mint() with the rebasing token as validatorToken. 3. Trigger negative rebase (reduce balances by 10%). 4. Attempt burn() - expect revert due to insufficient actual balance. 5. Attempt executeFeeSwap - expect revert on validatorToken.transfer. 6. Verify positive rebase scenario: trigger positive rebase, confirm excess tokens are inaccessible (no way to withdraw them).
uncertainties: The protocol may intend to only support standard non-rebasing stablecoins, but this is not enforced in code
The severity depends on whether rebasing tokens are realistically expected to be used with this AMM
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test with mock rebasing tokens to verify exact revert conditions
Test edge case where rebase makes balance exactly equal to reserves (no excess, no deficit)
Test interaction between multiple pools sharing the same rebasing token
